live_catalyst_narrative: The most likely driver was company-specific news plus broader market pressure, not a SEBI filing or an obvious insider/block-deal event. The clearest trigger in recent reporting was Reliance’s denial of a Bloomberg story about Russian crude shipments to Jamnagar, which coincided with a sharp intraday fall on heavy volumes.economictimes+1What appears to have caused itReliance said its Jamnagar refinery had not received Russian oil cargoes in the prior three weeks and did not expect any in January, after a Bloomberg report suggested otherwise. Reuters reported the drop followed that clarification and also cited pressure from Trump-related tariff concerns and weakness in retail sentiment across the sector. That makes the move look more like a news shock amplified by market sentiment than a pure company-operations miss.reuters+1SEBI, insider, and block-deal checkFrom the available recent information, I did not find evidence of a fresh SEBI action, management change, or earnings miss explaining the move. I also did not see a recent block-deal report in the surfaced results, and the insider-transaction pages available did not show a clear new insider dump in the last 48 hours that would explain an abrupt 4% selloff. The data that did surface points more to older or general insider activity, not a decisive recent catalyst.finance.yahoo+4Retail panic or institutional sellingThe “heavy volumes” and index-drag language suggest institutional distribution or systematic selling likely played a role, but the catalyst itself still looks news-driven. Retail panic often appears after an initial institutional-led move, especially in a heavyweight name like Reliance that can pressure the index and invite momentum selling. So the best read is: news-triggered decline, then accelerated by broader market/sector weakness and high-volume selling.economictimes+1Practical readIf you are trading this name intraday, watch whether the fall happened immediately after the news hit and whether volume spiked without recovery on bounce attempts. That pattern would favor institutional selling or de-risking, while a fast V-shaped reversal would suggest mostly retail overreaction.
